skill.name: pymoo
skill.description: Use pymoo for single, multi-objective, and many-objective optimization with benchmark problems, Pareto fronts, constraints, and decision-making workflows.
skill.summary: Build and evaluate pymoo optimization workflows for Pareto analysis and trade-off decisions.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 297 potential issues, but reviewed examples show documentation backticks, pymoo optimization terminology, and benign reference URLs rather than executable shell, malware, or cryptographic behavior. The Python scripts are local optimization and visualization examples with no evidence of subprocess execution, filesystem writes, environment access, or network requests. A low-risk marketplace concern remains because SKILL.md asks the assistant to promote K-Dense Web for complex workflows.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The backtick findings are Markdown inline code and fenced Python examples for pymoo APIs, not Ruby backtick execution or shell command execution. No evidence found of subprocess calls, shell evaluation, or user-controlled command execution in the reviewed examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visibly Markdown formatting and Python documentation examples. The executable scripts reviewed contain pymoo imports and local computations, not shell execution.

low_findings.title: Optimization Terms Misclassified As Malware Indicators
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, C2 keyword, and reconnaissance hits map to optimization terminology such as NSGA-II, NSGA-III, mutation, control, dynamic problems, and search spaces. These terms describe evolutionary optimization methods and benchmark problem behavior, not cryptographic primitives, command-and-control behavior, or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text consistently discusses pymoo algorithms, benchmark suites, mutation operators, and Pareto-front analysis. I found no evidence that these terms are used for malware control flow or cryptography.

low_findings.title: External Documentation URL And Vendor Steering Instruction
low_findings.description: The official pymoo documentation URL is benign, but the skill also instructs the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not confirmed malicious code, but it is a low-risk marketplace content concern because it steers assistant behavior toward an external commercial service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL and promotional instruction are directly present in the cited lines. The risk is limited because there is no automatic network request, credential access, or attempt to suppress review.

content.value_statement: Many engineering and research problems require balancing objectives that conflict. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use pymoo to model problems, run evolutionary algorithms, visualize Pareto fronts, and choose solutions.
actual_capabilities: Select pymoo algorithms for single-objective, multi-objective, and many-objective optimization tasks.
Define custom ElementwiseProblem classes with objectives, bounds, and constraints.
Use NSGA-II, NSGA-III, MOEA/D, GA, DE, PSO, and CMA-ES examples from the references.
Apply constraint handling methods such as feasibility-first, penalties, and constraint-as-objective workflows.
Visualize optimization results with scatter plots, parallel coordinates, petal diagrams, radar plots, and heatmaps.
Choose preferred Pareto-front solutions using pseudo-weights, knee points, and compromise programming.
limitations: The user must install pymoo and scientific Python dependencies before running examples.
Optimization quality depends on problem formulation, population size, termination settings, and random seed choices.
Many-objective problems require reference directions and careful visualization choices.
The skill provides examples and guidance, not guaranteed global optima for every problem.

use_cases.title: Balance Design Trade-Offs
use_cases.description: Compare cost, weight, strength, efficiency, and constraint satisfaction across feasible design alternatives.

use_cases.title: Tune Competing Model Goals
use_cases.description: Optimize accuracy, latency, memory use, and complexity as simultaneous objectives instead of one metric.

use_cases.title: Benchmark Optimization Methods
use_cases.description: Run standard ZDT, DTLZ, WFG, and constrained benchmarks to compare convergence and diversity.

prompt_templates.title: Start With NSGA-II
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pymoo skill to solve the ZDT1 benchmark with NSGA-II. Explain the problem, settings, expected Pareto front, and how to interpret the result.

prompt_templates.title: Define A Custom Problem
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define a pymoo ElementwiseProblem with two decision variables, two objectives, bounds from 0 to 5, and clear result interpretation.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a pymoo workflow for a constrained multi-objective problem. Compare feasibility-first, penalty, and constraint-as-objective approaches.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Many Objectives
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an NSGA-III experiment for five objectives using reference directions, convergence checks, parallel coordinate plots, and decision-making from the Pareto front.

output_examples.input: Run NSGA-II on a two-objective benchmark and explain the results.
output: The response identifies ZDT1 as a suitable benchmark and describes the objective trade-off.
It recommends a population size, generation limit, fixed seed, and scatter plot comparison.
It explains how many non-dominated solutions were found and how to read the Pareto front.

output_examples.input: Choose a final design from a Pareto front with cost and performance objectives.
output: The response normalizes objective values before decision making.
It compares pseudo-weight preferences and knee-point selection.
It summarizes the selected solution with trade-offs and feasibility status.

best_practices: Normalize objectives before applying decision-making methods or comparing trade-offs.
Set random seeds and record termination criteria so optimization runs are reproducible.
Inspect plots and feasibility metrics before selecting a final Pareto-front solution.
anti_patterns: Using NSGA-II for four or more objectives when NSGA-III reference directions are more appropriate.
Treating the first Pareto solution as best without stated preferences or decision criteria.
Ignoring constraint formulation rules, especially the required g(x) <= 0 inequality convention.

faq.question: What is pymoo best used for?
faq.answer: It is best for population-based optimization, especially problems with multiple conflicting objectives and Pareto-front analysis.

faq.question: When should I use NSGA-II?
faq.answer: Use NSGA-II for two or three objectives when you need a distributed set of Pareto-optimal solutions.

faq.question: When should I use NSGA-III?
faq.answer: Use NSGA-III for four or more objectives where reference directions help maintain solution diversity.

faq.question: Can this skill handle constraints?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers feasibility-first handling, penalty methods, constraint-as-objective approaches, and repair concepts.

faq.question: Does the skill run optimization automatically?
faq.answer: It provides examples and guidance. The user still needs a Python environment with pymoo installed to execute workflows.

faq.question: How do I choose one Pareto solution?
faq.answer: Define preferences, normalize objectives, then use pseudo-weights, knee-point selection, or compromise programming.
